event: The Pietà (Lamentation over the Dead Christ)
symbols: Christ's lifeless body on Mary's lap — the Incarnate Word returned to the mother who bore him, the cost of redemption
Mary's sorrowful gaze — Simeon's prophecy fulfilled: 'a sword will pierce your own soul' (Luke 2:35)
open landscape with distant city — Jerusalem, the city that kills the prophets, visible beyond the scene of death
bare tree at left — the tree of the cross, the new Tree of Life stripped bare
Christ's wound marks — the price paid for human sin, the marks of sacrificial love
description: In an open landscape, a woman in a purple robe and white head covering sits on the ground, cradling the limp body of a dead man across her lap. The man is nearly nude, with a loincloth, and bears visible marks on his hands and side. His head falls back, eyes closed. The woman gazes down at him with an expression of restrained grief. Behind them, a broad green landscape extends to distant towns and pale blue mountains under a clear sky. A bare tree stands at the left edge.
theological_description: Bellini's Pietà presents the most intimate moment of the Passion — the dead Christ held by his grieving mother. This scene, not explicitly described in the Gospels but deeply embedded in Christian devotion, follows the deposition from the cross (John 19:38-40). Mary, who said 'yes' to God at the Annunciation (Luke 1:38), now holds the lifeless result of that 'yes.' Simeon's prophecy at the Presentation finds its piercing fulfillment: 'a sword will pierce your own soul also' (Luke 2:35). The serene landscape behind the grief creates a deliberate tension — creation continues its course while its Creator lies dead, fulfilling Isaiah's word: 'He was cut off from the land of the living; for the transgression of my people he was stricken' (Isaiah 53:8). Yet the open sky and bright horizon hint at what lies beyond this death — the Resurrection. The visible wound in Christ's side (John 19:34) from which flowed blood and water is the sacramental fountain from which the Church is born. This is the paradox of the cross: 'It is finished' (John 19:30) is not defeat but the completion of salvation.

scripture_refs.reason: The deposition from the cross and preparation of Christ's body — the narrative moment preceding this scene
Simeon's prophecy that a sword would pierce Mary's soul — fulfilled in her grief over the dead Christ
The Suffering Servant cut off from the land of the living for the transgression of the people
'It is finished' — the completion of Christ's redemptive work visible in his lifeless body
The wound in Christ's side from which flowed blood and water — visible in the painting
